questions.question: Why can asynchronous pipelines hide overload better than synchronous systems?
options: Because buffering can absorb pressure for a while, delaying visible failure even as backlog grows dangerously.
Because queues always process work faster than producers can send it.
Because lag is impossible to measure in event systems.
Because async systems cannot fail closed.
questions.explanation: Buffers smooth bursts but can also mask growing failure.

questions.question: What backlog signal is often more actionable than raw queue depth alone?
options: Oldest unprocessed item age or user-visible completion delay.
The number of markdown files in the repository.
The number of fields in the event payload.
Only broker host uptime.
questions.explanation: Age maps better to user impact and hidden overload than count alone.

questions.question: Which design is strongest under mixed critical and best-effort traffic?
options: Protect critical work with priority lanes or admission control instead of making every event compete equally.
Let all classes share one undifferentiated queue and hope autoscaling keeps up.
Replay old promotional traffic first because it arrived earlier.
Avoid defining overload behavior until the first major incident.
questions.explanation: Differentiated policies are often necessary when business value differs.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of focusing only on queue depth?
options: A large queue may be acceptable briefly, while a smaller queue with old items may already be violating user expectations.
Queue depth always underestimates storage cost by definition.
Depth makes consumer autoscaling impossible.
Depth cannot be exported as a metric.
questions.explanation: Age and service delay often matter more than count alone.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with bounded queues or bounded backlog age?
options: You surface overload earlier, but you may need to reject, defer, or shed lower-value work.
You gain infinite burst absorption with no product impact.
You eliminate the need for producer feedback loops.
You make replay free.
questions.explanation: Boundaries force explicit overload policy instead of hiding it.

questions.question: Which metric most directly shows whether the system is recovering?
options: Drain rate relative to ingest rate and the trend in oldest backlog age.
Only the number of configured partitions.
The total count of dashboards.
Average line length in logs.
questions.explanation: Recovery means the system is draining faster than new work arrives.

questions.question: Why can replay trigger a second incident?
options: Because catch-up traffic can compete with live work and overload already-fragile downstream systems.
Because replay automatically disables deduplication.
Because replay makes brokers forget offsets.
Because replay cannot be rate-limited.
questions.explanation: Recovery traffic itself can become overload if not controlled.

questions.question: What question matters most before introducing producer throttling?
options: Whether producers can safely slow down, batch, or degrade without violating critical product promises.
Whether the queue names are short enough.
Whether JSON or Avro is more popular internally.
Whether the consumers are deployed on virtual machines.
questions.explanation: Throttling is only safe if product behavior under slow publish is understood.
